96ad4976-912b-4e8c-878f-ee6a10d2f815.case_name: Nyathi v Pioneer Africa Limited (647 of 2024 LCMT272005) 2026 ZWBHC 49 (24 February 2026).docx
96ad4976-912b-4e8c-878f-ee6a10d2f815.hearing_date: The applicant, after a protracted labour dispute, obtained judgment under cover of case number LC/MT/96/01. The Labour Court finalized the dispute on 15 November 2005 in judgment number LC/MT/27/2005. The applicant was granted the following relief:
96ad4976-912b-4e8c-878f-ee6a10d2f815.summary: MPOKISENG DUBE J:  This is an application to revive a judgment of the Labour Court. The applicant seeks relief as follows:
96ad4976-912b-4e8c-878f-ee6a10d2f815.raw_text:
CHARLES NYATHI                                                                            
Versus
PIONEER AFRICA LIMITED (now known 
As Unifreight Africa Limited)                                                           
HIGH COURT OF ZIMBABWE
MPOKISENG DUBE J
BULAWAYO 3 MARCH 2025, & 24 FEBRUARY 2026
Court Application to revive a judgment 
J. Tshuma, for the applicant
 L. Matapura, for the respondent
MPOKISENG DUBE J:  This is an application to revive a judgment of the Labour Court. The applicant seeks relief as follows:
The Judgement under the cover of case number LC/MT/96/01, handed down by the Labour Court on the 15th  of November 2005 under judgement number LC/MT/27/2005, be and is hereby revived as an executable order of that court.
The respondent is hereby ordered to pay the costs of this application on a legal practitioner and own client scale.
Brief Background
The applicant, after a protracted labour dispute, obtained judgment under cover of case number LC/MT/96/01. The Labour Court finalized the dispute on 15 November 2005 in judgment number LC/MT/27/2005. The applicant was granted the following relief:
(a) Respondent shall reinstate appellant without loss of salary and benefits, OR
(b) Respondent shall pay appellant damages for loss of employment in an agreed
amount failing, which either party may apply to this Court for assessment of
the said damages; 
The respondent having complied with none of the above orders of court despite demand, the matter went into abeyance for about 19 years. I shall not discuss the reasons proffered, as that goes to the merits of the matter. Suffice to say, at the hearing of this application, I directed the parties to first deal with the preliminary points taken by the respondent. After argument on such points, I directed that the parties attend on the 19th March, wherein I would deliver my ruling. Before that, the applicant launched a fresh application under a separate case number seeking directions from this court on how to proceed, as they felt they had been ambushed with a point of law, i.e., on the correct identity of the respondent herein. The applicant felt he needed time to investigate the true identity of the respondent and make further submissions to the court before a ruling could be handed down. That application was opposed and led to an equally protracted contestation. That matter was ultimately heard and disposed of on the 25th November 2025.
Preliminary Points Taken
 I now proceed to deliver my ruling on the preliminary points raised by Mr. Matapura as follows:
Application improperly before the court, alternatively, that this court has no jurisdiction to entertain the application.
Under this head respondent argued that in terms of the 2013 Constitution of our land, and the High Court Rules 2021, the Labour Court and the High Court only interact with each other in one instance, i.e., at registration of its judgments. With this view, he argued that this court is non-suited to entertain an application for revival of a Labour court judgment. He contended that it is not appropriate to rely on this court’s inherent jurisdiction. He narrowed his argument to three sub-heads, viz.
that there is no law, either statute or common law, that permits such procedure.
He relied on the judgment of CHITAPI J, in the matter of Klasid Investment (Pvt) Ltd v Live Investment (Pvt) Ltd HH228-24
that because of the amendment to the 1971 Rules, especially Rule 69, the position is now that High Court Judgments do not superannuate for up to 30 years, and in casu, the 30-year period had not yet lapsed. For that reason, he contended that this application is improper.
That when seeking revival, it is compulsory to state that you seek a writ. He referred to Rule 69 of the High Court Rules 2021. He argued that in casu the applicant seeks to revive the second leg of the Labour Court Judgment, which, if successful, he still ought to seek quantification of damages in lieu of reinstatement back at the Labour Court. He argued that a writ is not in sight, and this can't be an appropriate forum.
That the application is predicated on a serious yet material non-disclosure.
Counsel argued that a party who approaches the court on motion proceedings must do so with all candour. He further refined his argument into two sub-heads, viz.
That the applicant did not tell the court that he was at the labour court seeking the same relief, i.e., revival of the same order. That he failed to file heads, and the matter was deemed abandoned. And that had he done so, this court would have realised that he is forum shopping.
That he was advised in the year 2002 that he would not be reinstated and that he ought to have taken action then. For that reason, his matter is now prescribed.
That the causa has prescribed in terms of the Labour Act.
Counsel argued that whatever its complexion this matter remains an unfair labour practice. Relief therefore ought to have been sought within 2 years. Since he has not done so he is a victim of extinctive prescription.
That there is no entity at law existing by the name of Unifreight Holdings Limited.
Counsel argued that in casu, Unifreight is completely mis cited in its old or new form. He argued that the applicant cannot even seek to amend the citation of the respondent before the High Court, yet proceedings were before the Labour Court. Counsel cited several case law authorities to buttress his argument both in the Supreme Court and in the High Court.
Opposition by the Applicant.
Counsel for the applicant did not follow the sequence adopted by Counsel for the respondent in responding to the preliminary objections taken. 
Ad Material non-disclosure  
Mr. Tshuma referred the court to page 9 of the record, particularly para 20. It reads as follows:
“20. Having failed to reach a consensual settlement of this issue with the respondent, I approached the Labour Court with an application to revive the judgment under cover of case number LC/MT/96/01 and judgment number LC/MT/27/2005 handed down by the Labour Court on the 16™ of December 2005. The respondent opposed that application and raised a preliminary point that the Labour Court, being a court limited by statute, had no jurisdiction to revive a superannuated judgment because such a claim is premised on the common law.”
Counsel then went on to argue that after this disclosure they further stated that they withdrew the application for revival in the Labour Court as they conceded that they were seeking a common law remedy before a court limited by statute. He made reference to page 78 of the record containing such submission. He further referred to Annexure “X” attached to its founding affidavit. He argued further that after the Labour Court accepted and endorsed the withdrawal it was not necessary to file Heads. For that reason, that matter cannot be correctly deemed abandoned. He contended that the point is ill taken and should fail.
Ad Prescription
Counsel Tshuma argued that it is a misplaced argument to aver that a failure to enforce a judgment of the year 2005 within a period of 2 years renders it prescribed. He argued that, instead the prescription of a court order is regulated by section 15 of the Prescription Act. Under that provision, he argued, it is clear, a court order prescribes after 30 years.
He argued that in casu, there is a court order which is a command for the respondent to act in a certain way i.e to reinstate the applicant failing which the parties should agree on a quantum of damages in lieu thereof. It is the administrative act which is being brought before this court not a plea to resolve a labour dispute between the parties. If anything, this court is being asked to exercise its administrative oversight in order to enable enforcement of the order.
He argued further that by definition prescription extinguishes a right, yet superannuation is a common law principle which says if you do not enforce your order within 3 years, you cannot thereafter do so without having obtained an order reviving it. In other words, a prescribed court order can not be revived yet a superannuated one can be revived so that it can be enforced.
Counsel then chronicled the history of the principle of superannuation as having been part of our law by reason of both the common law and the High Court Rules. He cited Order 40 Rule 448 of the 1971 Rules as having provided that an order of the court superannuates after 6 years. He argued further that the rule was repealed but the principle of superannuation was retained in our law by the 2013 Constitution, which provides that the common law in Zimbabwe is the Roman-Dutch Law as applied in the Cape of Good Hope.
He concluded by arguing that superannuation is a common law principle and not a creature of a statutory provision. It is retained as such by the Constitution. Therefore, in terms of the common law, an order superannuates after 3 years. If 30 years of prescription has not lapsed the order is still extant and the only way to revive it is via revival.
Ad Lack of Jurisdiction or Wrong Forum.
Mr. Tshuma argued further that the question that then arises is: to which court does one apply for revival of a superannuated court order? He submitted that he agrees with all case law cited by counsel Matapura to the effect that the Labour Court is of limited jurisdiction and has an exclusive right to adjudicate on purely labour matters to the exclusion of other courts. He accepts further that the Labour Court is a specialized court as created by the Constitution.
He interpreted this to mean the Labour Court cannot adjudicate on any matter that it is not specifically empowered by the Labour Act. He pursed his argument further to say the question one needs to ask oneself is:
Does the Labour Act allow it to revive its own judgments? The answer he contends is “No”. It does not have such powers.
Does the principle of superannuation apply to labour matters? And the answer thereto is “Yes, it does.”
Does it then mean a party with a superannuated judgment is without a remedy? The answer he submits is “No”. The party has to approach the High Court. It is the court with inherent jurisdiction to deal with all civil and criminal matters which are not prohibited by any statute. In casu there is no provision to approach the Labour Court, so the remedy is to approach the High Court as the court with inherent jurisdiction. He relied on the judgment per MAKARAU J (as she then was) in the matter of Sibanda & Anor Chinemhute N.O. & Anor HH 131-04 wherein she remarked that: 
 “Where a point of entry is hitherto non-existent for a member of the public in the form of procedure- one is inherently created in the interests of justice. This is a court of inherent jurisdiction.”
He concluded on this point by submitting that Counsel Matapura conflated the two principles of superannuation and prescription.    He made reference to the requirements that a litigant must satisfy to successfully mount a case for the revival of a superannuated judgment. He cited Mafoko v Alcatel Lucent South Africa (Pvt) Ltd and Another, 2015 ZALCTHB 240, where it was stated that;
“It is trite law that a court has the discretion to either abide to an application for revival or to refuse it. A court will not revive an old judgment if on the facts before it such revival would be futile, will only lead to useless litigation, and would not give the applicant any real remedy. It therefore follows that in order for the present application for revival of the judgment to succeed it must be shown the judgment debt remains outstanding, either in whole or in part. Otherwise, revival of judgment would be futile and academic.”
His point is, he submitted, that the requirements for reviving a superannuated judgment are quite distinct from the ones for prescription.  
Ad Wrong citation of the respondent
Mr. Tshuma argued that this point was raised for the first time in the history of litigation between the parties. He argued that such is prejudicial to the applicant as he is not given an opportunity to carry out his own investigations and answer to that.
He argued further that one ought to raise such a point at the first opportune time, i.e., at the Labour Court. He contended that this same citation was used in the labour court, and the respondent came and defended the claim through its agents, gave evidence under oath, and answered to the applicant’s claim. Respondent never objected to that claim. For the past 19 years, they engaged the applicant and never raised that objection. The present matter is just an application to revive the order of the court so that the matter can revert to the Labour Court for quantification of damages. This court is tasked simply with the task of reviving the order of the Labour Court and referring it back to the relevant court for quantification of damages. He argued that it is for the labour court to deal with the issue of citation of the respondent, and it is open for the applicant to raise his defences at that platform. For now, the respondent is estopped from raising that argument.
Ad Costs
Mr. Tshuma argued that since applicant was never informed that he would not be re-instated he is entitled to make an application as the current applicant to protect his rights, therefore, there is no need to penalise him with any punitive costs, even the preliminary points were to be upheld. 
Replication by Counsel for the Respondent
Mr. Matapura replicated to the effect that the preliminary point of mis-citation of the respondent is a point of law and can be raised at any time. He relied on the authority of Garia Safaris, Mukarati, Marage Resources and Delta Beverages matters.
He argued that in casu there was no application for amendment and therefore none can be granted.
He argued that the principle that this court has powers to regulate its own process simply means the discretion is limited to judgments made by itself, not any other court. He argued that if the logic propounded by the applicant’s counsel is accepted then it means the High Court has inherent powers to revive the Supreme and Constitutional Court orders.
He argued that the repeal of the 1971 Rules and failure to provide a similar provision in the 2021 Rules simply meant the legislature created a lacuna which ought to be addressed by the same legislature.
He argued further that a failure to comply with a Labour Court order amounted to an unfair labour practice. The remedy for such would be to approach the Labour officer within 2 years. He contended that in our jurisdiction prescription depends on the parent Act so if section 3 of the Labour Act stipulates 3 years, that ought to be the apex. The argument about the 30-year prescription does not apply. 
Mr. Matapura further argued that the respondent is not estopped from raising the mis-citation argument. He argued that a nullity is a nullity even without a court order and applicant therefore has no respondent before the Court. He persisted with a prayer for the upholding of the preliminary points he took on behalf of the respondent.
The Law Juxtaposed to the Preliminary Points raised.
Ad Jurisdiction
While I agree with Counsel Matapura in his argument that the only time the High Court has an interface with the Labour Court is at registration and enforcement stage of its judgments, I do not accept that a party who finds his / her judgment superannuated is without a remedy. The Labour Court as a creature of statute, enjoys no such powers however revival of a judgment is essentially part of its enforcement process. I accept the reasoning of MAKARAU J (as she then was) in the cited matter of Martin Sibanda & Anor v Bearson Chinemhute N.O. & Anor HH 131-04 and the matter of Cassimjee v Minister of Finance (455/11) [2012] ZASCA 101 (June 2012) to the effect that;
“The High Court has inherent power both at common law and in terms of section (s173) to regulate its own process….”
I say so from the logic that the Labour Court is simply a specialised division of the same High Court. While it may not have powers prescribed in its specialised form as the Labour court, it retains such power in its original form. As indeed Counsel Matapura avers, the repeal of the 1971 Rules at Order 40 Rule 448 and failure to provide a similar provision in the 2021 Rules meant the legislature created a lacuna. In the Supreme Court matters of S v Gumbura SC25-2021 and Barbarosa De Sa v Barbarosa De Sa SC34-2016 the common principle enunciated therein is that a court of inherent jurisdiction has default powers which it can exercise in the absence of express power and can deal with all areas of law and all procedural matters involving the administration of justice.
In casu I am convinced that the court has jurisdiction over the application made.
Ad Material non-disclosure
I take note that during his replication Counsel Matapura did not deal with this point. I take it to mean he concedes that indeed full and sufficient disclosure was made of the history of this matter in applicant’s founding affidavit. If counsel did not intend to make this concession, I nonetheless find that disclosure was made and in fact that a similar claim filed in the Labour Court was withdrawn with a tender of costs. It is therefore not correct that it was deemed abandoned. I accordingly dismiss this preliminary point.
Ad Prescription
I do not accept the view by counsel Matapura that an application for revival of a superannuated judgment is on the same footing as a claim made under the head of an unfair labour practice. It submits to no argument that there is a court order that remains extant. This matter is therefore governed by section 15 of the Prescription Act at item (a) (ii), which provides as follows:
“The period of prescription of a debt shall be—
(a) thirty years, in the case of—
(i) a debt secured by mortgage bond
;(ii) a judgment debt;
(iii) a debt in respect of taxation imposed or levied by or under any enactment;
(iv) a debt owed to the State in respect of any tax, royalty, tribute, share of the profits or other similar charge or consideration payable in connection with the exploitation of or the right to win minerals or other substances;”
The preliminary point taken by counsel for the respondent is therefore ill taken and accordingly dismissed.
Ad Wrong Citation
While it is true that a point of law can be taken at any stage in litigation, I am mindful of the fact that the parties in their long history of litigation in the Labour Court spanning over the past 19 or so years, the respondent came to court, answered to that name and defended the claim. It made no issue out of its citation. It is however, not for this court to deal with the issue of the parties’ names or their citation. The ambit of this court is simply an application for the revival of a judgment. This court will deal with the parties as they appeared in the Labour Court. It is only the Labour court that can entertain an application for amendment or not, if this court accedes to the revival. For that reason, I refuse to deal with this preliminary point as it is raised in the wrong forum.
 	In the foregoing, the following disposition is made,
Disposition
All the preliminary points be and are hereby dismissed.
The matter to be set down for arguments on the merits.
Costs of suit shall be in the cause. 
Webb, Low & Barry Inc Ben Baron & Partners, applicant’s legal practitioners
Mafongoya and Matapura Law Practice, respondent’s legal practitioners

Suffice to say, at the hearing of this application, I directed the parties to first deal with the preliminary points taken by the respondent. After argument on such points, I directed that the parties attend on the 19th March, wherein I would deliver my ruling. Before that, the applicant launched a fresh application under a separate case number seeking directions from this court on how to proceed, as they felt they had been ambushed with a point of law, i.e., on the correct identity of the respondent herein. The applicant felt he needed time to investigate the true identity of the respondent and make further submissions to the court before a ruling could be handed down. That application was opposed and led to an equally protracted contestation. That matter was ultimately heard and disposed of on the 25th November 2025.
Preliminary Points Taken
 I now proceed to deliver my ruling on the preliminary points raised by Mr. Matapura as follows:
Application improperly before the court, alternatively, that this court has no jurisdiction to entertain the application.
Under this head respondent argued that in terms of the 2013 Constitution of our land, and the High Court Rules 2021, the Labour Court and the High Court only interact with each other in one instance, i.e., at registration of its judgments. With this view, he argued that this court is non-suited to entertain an application for revival of a Labour court judgment. He contended that it is not appropriate to rely on this court’s inherent jurisdiction. He narrowed his argument to three sub-heads, viz.
that there is no law, either statute or common law, that permits such procedure.
He relied on the judgment of CHITAPI J, in the matter of Klasid Investment (Pvt) Ltd v Live Investment (Pvt) Ltd HH228-24
that because of the amendment to the 1971 Rules, especially Rule 69, the position is now that High Court Judgments do not superannuate for up to 30 years, and in casu, the 30-year period had not yet lapsed. For that reason, he contended that this application is improper.
That when seeking revival, it is compulsory to state that you seek a writ. He referred to Rule 69 of the High Court Rules 2021. He argued that in casu the applicant seeks to revive the second leg of the Labour Court Judgment, which, if successful, he still ought to seek quantification of damages in lieu of reinstatement back at the Labour Court. He argued that a writ is not in sight, and this can't be an appropriate forum.
That the application is predicated on a serious yet material non-disclosure.
Counsel argued that a party who approaches the court on motion proceedings must do so with all candour. He further refined his argument into two sub-heads, viz.
That the applicant did not tell the court that he was at the labour court seeking the same relief, i.e., revival of the same order. That he failed to file heads, and the matter was deemed abandoned. And that had he done so, this court would have realised that he is forum shopping.
That he was advised in the year 2002 that he would not be reinstated and that he ought to have taken action then. For that reason, his matter is now prescribed.
That the causa has prescribed in terms of the Labour Act.
Counsel argued that whatever its complexion this matter remains an unfair labour practice. Relief therefore ought to have been sought within 2 years. Since he has not done so he is a victim of extinctive prescription.
That there is no entity at law existing by the name of Unifreight Holdings Limited.
Counsel argued that in casu, Unifreight is completely mis cited in its old or new form. He argued that the applicant cannot even seek to amend the citation of the respondent before the High Court, yet proceedings were before the Labour Court. Counsel cited several case law authorities to buttress his argument both in the Supreme Court and in the High Court.
Opposition by the Applicant.
Counsel for the applicant did not follow the sequence adopted by Counsel for the respondent in responding to the preliminary objections taken. 
Ad Material non-disclosure  
Mr. Tshuma referred the court to page 9 of the record, particularly para 20. It reads as follows:
“20. Having failed to reach a consensual settlement of this issue with the respondent, I approached the Labour Court with an application to revive the judgment under cover of case number LC/MT/96/01 and judgment number LC/MT/27/2005 handed down by the Labour Court on the 16™ of December 2005. The respondent opposed that application and raised a preliminary point that the Labour Court, being a court limited by statute, had no jurisdiction to revive a superannuated judgment because such a claim is premised on the common law.”
Counsel then went on to argue that after this disclosure they further stated that they withdrew the application for revival in the Labour Court as they conceded that they were seeking a common law remedy before a court limited by statute. He made reference to page 78 of the record containing such submission. He further referred to Annexure “X” attached to its founding affidavit. He argued further that after the Labour Court accepted and endorsed the withdrawal it was not necessary to file Heads. For that reason, that matter cannot be correctly deemed abandoned. He contended that the point is ill taken and should fail.
Ad Prescription
Counsel Tshuma argued that it is a misplaced argument to aver that a failure to enforce a judgment of the year 2005 within a period of 2 years renders it prescribed. He argued that, instead the prescription of a court order is regulated by section 15 of the Prescription Act. Under that provision, he argued, it is clear, a court order prescribes after 30 years.
He argued that in casu, there is a court order which is a command for the respondent to act in a certain way i.e to reinstate the applicant failing which the parties should agree on a quantum of damages in lieu thereof. It is the administrative act which is being brought before this court not a plea to resolve a labour dispute between the parties. If anything, this court is being asked to exercise its administrative oversight in order to enable enforcement of the order.
He argued further that by definition prescription extinguishes a right, yet superannuation is a common law principle which says if you do not enforce your order within 3 years, you cannot thereafter do so without having obtained an order reviving it. In other words, a prescribed court order can not be revived yet a superannuated one can be revived so that it can be enforced.
Counsel then chronicled the history of the principle of superannuation as having been part of our law by reason of both the common law and the High Court Rules. He cited Order 40 Rule 448 of the 1971 Rules as having provided that an order of the court superannuates after 6 years. He argued further that the rule was repealed but the principle of superannuation was retained in our law by the 2013 Constitution, which provides that the common law in Zimbabwe is the Roman-Dutch Law as applied in the Cape of Good Hope.
He concluded by arguing that superannuation is a common law principle and not a creature of a statutory provision. It is retained as such by the Constitution. Therefore, in terms of the common law, an order superannuates after 3 years. If 30 years of prescription has not lapsed the order is still extant and the only way to revive it is via revival.
Ad Lack of Jurisdiction or Wrong Forum.
Mr. Tshuma argued further that the question that then arises is: to which court does one apply for revival of a superannuated court order? He submitted that he agrees with all case law cited by counsel Matapura to the effect that the Labour Court is of limited jurisdiction and has an exclusive right to adjudicate on purely labour matters to the exclusion of other courts. He accepts further that the Labour Court is a specialized court as created by the Constitution.
He interpreted this to mean the Labour Court cannot adjudicate on any matter that it is not specifically empowered by the Labour Act. He pursed his argument further to say the question one needs to ask oneself is:
Does the Labour Act allow it to revive its own judgments? The answer he contends is “No”. It does not have such powers.
Does the principle of superannuation apply to labour matters? And the answer thereto is “Yes, it does.”
Does it then mean a party with a superannuated judgment is without a remedy? The answer he submits is “No”. The party has to approach the High Court. It is the court with inherent jurisdiction to deal with all civil and criminal matters which are not prohibited by any statute. In casu there is no provision to approach the Labour Court, so the remedy is to approach the High Court as the court with inherent jurisdiction. He relied on the judgment per MAKARAU J (as she then was) in the matter of Sibanda & Anor Chinemhute N.O. & Anor HH 131-04 wherein she remarked that: 
 “Where a point of entry is hitherto non-existent for a member of the public in the form of procedure- one is inherently created in the interests of justice. This is a court of inherent jurisdiction.”
He concluded on this point by submitting that Counsel Matapura conflated the two principles of superannuation and prescription.    He made reference to the requirements that a litigant must satisfy to successfully mount a case for the revival of a superannuated judgment. He cited Mafoko v Alcatel Lucent South Africa (Pvt) Ltd and Another, 2015 ZALCTHB 240, where it was stated that;
“It is trite law that a court has the discretion to either abide to an application for revival or to refuse it. A court will not revive an old judgment if on the facts before it such revival would be futile, will only lead to useless litigation, and would not give the applicant any real remedy. It therefore follows that in order for the present application for revival of the judgment to succeed it must be shown the judgment debt remains outstanding, either in whole or in part. Otherwise, revival of judgment would be futile and academic.”
His point is, he submitted, that the requirements for reviving a superannuated judgment are quite distinct from the ones for prescription.  
Ad Wrong citation of the respondent
Mr. Tshuma argued that this point was raised for the first time in the history of litigation between the parties. He argued that such is prejudicial to the applicant as he is not given an opportunity to carry out his own investigations and answer to that.
He argued further that one ought to raise such a point at the first opportune time, i.e., at the Labour Court. He contended that this same citation was used in the labour court, and the respondent came and defended the claim through its agents, gave evidence under oath, and answered to the applicant’s claim. Respondent never objected to that claim. For the past 19 years, they engaged the applicant and never raised that objection. The present matter is just an application to revive the order of the court so that the matter can revert to the Labour Court for quantification of damages. This court is tasked simply with the task of reviving the order of the Labour Court and referring it back to the relevant court for quantification of damages. He argued that it is for the labour court to deal with the issue of citation of the respondent, and it is open for the applicant to raise his defences at that platform. For now, the respondent is estopped from raising that argument.
Ad Costs
Mr. Tshuma argued that since applicant was never informed that he would not be re-instated he is entitled to make an application as the current applicant to protect his rights, therefore, there is no need to penalise him with any punitive costs, even the preliminary points were to be upheld. 
Replication by Counsel for the Respondent
Mr. Matapura replicated to the effect that the preliminary point of mis-citation of the respondent is a point of law and can be raised at any time. He relied on the authority of Garia Safaris, Mukarati, Marage Resources and Delta Beverages matters.
He argued that in casu there was no application for amendment and therefore none can be granted.
He argued that the principle that this court has powers to regulate its own process simply means the discretion is limited to judgments made by itself, not any other court. He argued that if the logic propounded by the applicant’s counsel is accepted then it means the High Court has inherent powers to revive the Supreme and Constitutional Court orders.
He argued that the repeal of the 1971 Rules and failure to provide a similar provision in the 2021 Rules simply meant the legislature created a lacuna which ought to be addressed by the same legislature.
He argued further that a failure to comply with a Labour Court order amounted to an unfair labour practice. The remedy for such would be to approach the Labour officer within 2 years. He contended that in our jurisdiction prescription depends on the parent Act so if section 3 of the Labour Act stipulates 3 years, that ought to be the apex. The argument about the 30-year prescription does not apply. 
Mr. Matapura further argued that the respondent is not estopped from raising the mis-citation argument. He argued that a nullity is a nullity even without a court order and applicant therefore has no respondent before the Court. He persisted with a prayer for the upholding of the preliminary points he took on behalf of the respondent.
The Law Juxtaposed to the Preliminary Points raised.
Ad Jurisdiction
While I agree with Counsel Matapura in his argument that the only time the High Court has an interface with the Labour Court is at registration and enforcement stage of its judgments, I do not accept that a party who finds his / her judgment superannuated is without a remedy. The Labour Court as a creature of statute, enjoys no such powers however revival of a judgment is essentially part of its enforcement process. I accept the reasoning of MAKARAU J (as she then was) in the cited matter of Martin Sibanda & Anor v Bearson Chinemhute N.O. & Anor HH 131-04 and the matter of Cassimjee v Minister of Finance (455/11) [2012] ZASCA 101 (June 2012) to the effect that;
“The High Court has inherent power both at common law and in terms of section (s173) to regulate its own process….”
I say so from the logic that the Labour Court is simply a specialised division of the same High Court. While it may not have powers prescribed in its specialised form as the Labour court, it retains such power in its original form. As indeed Counsel Matapura avers, the repeal of the 1971 Rules at Order 40 Rule 448 and failure to provide a similar provision in the 2021 Rules meant the legislature created a lacuna. In the Supreme Court matters of S v Gumbura SC25-2021 and Barbarosa De Sa v Barbarosa De Sa SC34-2016 the common principle enunciated therein is that a court of inherent jurisdiction has default powers which it can exercise in the absence of express power and can deal with all areas of law and all procedural matters involving the administration of justice.
In casu I am convinced that the court has jurisdiction over the application made.
Ad Material non-disclosure
I take note that during his replication Counsel Matapura did not deal with this point. I take it to mean he concedes that indeed full and sufficient disclosure was made of the history of this matter in applicant’s founding affidavit. If counsel did not intend to make this concession, I nonetheless find that disclosure was made and in fact that a similar claim filed in the Labour Court was withdrawn with a tender of costs. It is therefore not correct that it was deemed abandoned. I accordingly dismiss this preliminary point.
Ad Prescription
I do not accept the view by counsel Matapura that an application for revival of a superannuated judgment is on the same footing as a claim made under the head of an unfair labour practice. It submits to no argument that there is a court order that remains extant. This matter is therefore governed by section 15 of the Prescription Act at item (a) (ii), which provides as follows:
“The period of prescription of a debt shall be—
(a) thirty years, in the case of—
(i) a debt secured by mortgage bond
;(ii) a judgment debt;
(iii) a debt in respect of taxation imposed or levied by or under any enactment;
(iv) a debt owed to the State in respect of any tax, royalty, tribute, share of the profits or other similar charge or consideration payable in connection with the exploitation of or the right to win minerals or other substances;”
The preliminary point taken by counsel for the respondent is therefore ill taken and accordingly dismissed.
Ad Wrong Citation
While it is true that a point of law can be taken at any stage in litigation, I am mindful of the fact that the parties in their long history of litigation in the Labour Court spanning over the past 19 or so years, the respondent came to court, answered to that name and defended the claim. It made no issue out of its citation. It is however, not for this court to deal with the issue of the parties’ names or their citation. The ambit of this court is simply an application for the revival of a judgment. This court will deal with the parties as they appeared in the Labour Court. It is only the Labour court that can entertain an application for amendment or not, if this court accedes to the revival. For that reason, I refuse to deal with this preliminary point as it is raised in the wrong forum.
 	In the foregoing, the following disposition is made,
Disposition
All the preliminary points be and are hereby dismissed.
The matter to be set down for arguments on the merits.
Costs of suit shall be in the cause. 
Webb, Low & Barry Inc Ben Baron & Partners, applicant’s legal practitioners
Mafongoya and Matapura Law Practice, respondent’s legal practitioners

cf1c10b8-7e3e-4955-8867-1b145bd0d76b.summary: MUNANGATI- MANONGWA J: The plaintiff herein issued Summons against the defendant seeking an order declaring the contract of employment signed by the Plaintiff and Defendant on the 14th day of December 2024 null and void and also wants costs of suit. cf1c10b8-7e3e-4955-8867-1b145bd0d76b.raw_text:
TAWANDA RAYMOND CHINEMBIRI
versus
STAGRI BRANDS (PRIVATE) LIMITED
HIGH COURT OF ZIMBABWE
MUNANGATI- MANONGWA J
HARARE, 10 February 2026
Opposed Matter
M. M Masitera, for the plaintiff
M Moyo, for the defendant
                  MUNANGATI- MANONGWA J: The plaintiff herein issued Summons against the defendant seeking an order declaring the contract of employment signed by the Plaintiff and Defendant on the 14th day of December 2024 null and void and also wants costs of suit. The Defendant raised a Special Plea in Bar challenging this court’s jurisdiction to entertain the Plaintiff’s claim. The Defendant contends that the dispute is a purely labour matter falling within the exclusive jurisdiction of the Labour Court. The Plaintiff opposes the Special Plea.
The court is therefore called upon to determine whether it has jurisdiction to hear a claim for declaratory relief concerning the validity of a written fixed-term contract of employment allegedly procured through undue influence and coercion.
Factual Background
The material facts are largely common cause and have been fully set out in the pleadings. In essence, the Plaintiff alleges that he was employed by the Defendant under a four-year verbal fixed-term contract commencing in April 2024. In December 2024, following the successful disbursement of funding secured with the assistance of the Plaintiff, the Defendant induced him to sign a four-month written contract under conditions of undue pressure, misrepresentation, and economic duress. The Plaintiff alleges that he initially declined to sign the new contract, pointing out that he was already under a four-year contract which had not yet lapsed. He alleges that the Defendant, acting through its representative, one Valentine Tapfumaneyi, misrepresented to the Plaintiff that the company was undergoing a system upgrade and that, for administrative reasons, it was necessary for the plaintiff to sign a temporary contract terminating on 31 December 2024. 
The plaintiff alleges that the Defendant further placed undue pressure on him to sign the contract by withholding all his accrued arrear salary and only undertook to release same after the Plaintiff had signed the contract. It is the plaintiff’s case that he was assured that a new contract covering the remainder of the original four-year term would be issued effective January 2025. Thus, plaintiff maintains that Defendant, by virtue of its dominant position as employer, unduly influenced his decision-making, thereby rendering his will pliable and his resistance weakened. He contends that the Defendant exploited this influence unscrupulously to procure the Plaintiff’s consent. The plaintiff alleges that fearing to lose his job and the income he so needed he signed the shortened contract under undue pressure exerted on him. It is the plaintiff case that contrary to the defendant’s undertaking to sign a new contract in January 2025 the defendant terminated his employment relying on the fixed term contract.
	The Plaintiff thus now seeks a declaratory order that the four-month written contract is invalid at common law. It is common cause that the Plaintiff has also approached a Labour Officer alleging unfair dismissal arising from the termination of his employment.
	In raising the special plea the defendant argues that:
The dispute arises from an employment relationship;
Employment disputes fall within the exclusive jurisdiction of the Labour Court;
The Plaintiff has already invoked labour dispute resolution mechanisms in respect of the same employment relationship.
	In addition, the Defendant contends that the Plaintiff is guilty of material non-disclosure. It is argued that the Plaintiff failed to fully disclose that the dispute relating to the termination of his employment was pending before a Labour Officer, who is statutorily empowered to determine issues of unfair dismissal. The Defendant submits that this non-disclosure amounts to an abuse of court process and that the Plaintiff was forum shopping. Ms Moyo also argued that the plaintiff approached this court with “dirty hands.” On this basis, the Defendant argues that the High Court should decline jurisdiction and defer to the labour dispute resolution framework. However Ms Moyo for the defendant later abandoned the “dirty hands” argument given that there was no basis on which that allegation could be sustained.
Issues for Determination
	The sole issue for determination is whether this court has jurisdiction to grant declaratory relief concerning the validity of an employment contract allegedly procured through undue influence.
Analysis
	Apart from statutory provisions, it is trite that jurisdiction is determined by the nature of the relief sought. In Air Zimbabwe (Private) Limited v Mateko & Ors SC 180/20 the court held that jurisdiction is established by examining the plaintiff’s cause of action and the remedy sought. Similarly, in Tamanikwa & Others v Zimbabwe Manpower Development Fund SC 33/13, the Supreme Court reaffirmed that the Labour Court’s jurisdiction is limited to matters specifically conferred upon it by statute.
	The Plaintiff in casu does not seek reinstatement, damages for unfair dismissal, or any relief provided for under the Labour Act [Chapter 28:01]. Instead, he seeks a declaratory order on the validity of a contract, relying on common law doctrines of undue influence, coercion, and misrepresentation. The power to grant declaratory relief is vested in the High Court by virtue of its inherent jurisdiction. This position was clearly articulated in, Agribank v Machingaifa 2008 (1) ZLR 244 (SC) where the Supreme Court held that the High Court has wide powers to grant declaratory relief whenever there is an existing, future, or contingent right requiring de termination. The High Court Act further makes it clear where it provides in S14 that:
 14.  High Court may determine future or contingent rights
	“The High Court may, in its discretion, at the instance of any interested person, inquire into and 	determine any existing, future or contingent right or obligation, notwithstanding that such 	person cannot claim any relief consequential upon such 	determination.” 
	The Labour Court, being a creature of statute, has no inherent jurisdiction and cannot determine the validity of contracts at common law. This principle was emphasised in Surface Investments (Pvt) Ltd v Chinyani HH 295/14 where the court held that issues involving the existence, validity, or enforceability of contracts fall outside the exclusive domain of labour forums.
	The Defendant’s argument that the mere fact that the contract is one of employment automatically renders the dispute a labour matter is misplaced. In William Bains & Co. Holdings (Pvt) Ltd v Nyamukunda HH 309/13 it was held that the “Labour Court enjoys exclusivity in all matters where the cause of action and the remedy are all provided for in the Act. Outside that, for instance where the cause of action and the remedy are located in the common law, the ouster provision in the Labour Act has no application and this court will exercise jurisdiction.”
	Even where labour issues are implicated, the High Court retains jurisdiction to determine matters of contractual validity. In Munodawafa v Masvingo District Administrator & Ors HH 571/15 the court confirmed that the High Court’s jurisdiction is not lightly ousted and will only be excluded where legislation does so in clear and unambiguous terms.
	Turning to the argument of material non-disclosure, the court is not persuaded that this contention advances the Defendant’s Special Plea. The Plaintiff’s proceedings before a Labour Officer concern an allegation of unfair dismissal, a statutory cause of action falling under the Labour Act. The present proceedings concern the common law validity of a written contract allegedly procured through undue influence. These are distinct causes of action, grounded in different legal regimes, and seeking different relief. The Defendant did not raise a plea of lis pendens, nor did it demonstrate that the determination of the contractual validity is a matter that the Labour Officer is empowered to decide.  In any case the lack of jurisdiction to issue declaratory orders by the Labour Court would of necessity apply to a labour officer.
	Allegations of non-disclosure and “dirty hands,” even if established, do not oust this court’s jurisdiction and are irrelevant to the narrow jurisdictional enquiry raised by a Special Plea in Bar. In any event, parallel proceedings are permissible where the causes of action and relief sought are materially different.
Conclusion
	The Plaintiff’s claim is grounded in common law and seeks declaratory relief that is not available before the Labour Court or a Labour Officer. The matter is therefore not a purely labour dispute. This court has jurisdiction to hear and determine the claim.
	In that regard, the court makes the following order:
The Defendant’s Special Plea in Bar is hereby dismissed with costs.
MUNANGATI – MANONGWA J ………………………………..
M.Moyo, Legal Practice for the defendant
Chimwamurombe, Legal practice for the Plaintiff

80e8a0b6-001d-423d-b4b2-4522974256dc.filename: Marikanda v Makombe and Others (HCH114625 HCH114626) 2026 ZWHHC 196 (17 March 2026).docx
80e8a0b6-001d-423d-b4b2-4522974256dc.case_name: Marikanda v Makombe and Others (HCH114625 HCH114626) 2026 ZWHHC 196 (17 March 2026).docx
80e8a0b6-001d-423d-b4b2-4522974256dc.summary: [1] This is an urgent chamber application for an interim interdict, brought in terms of rule 60 of the High Court Rules, 2021, in which the applicant seeks an order stopping a disciplinary hearing pending the determination of a declaratory application lodged under case number HCH1145/26.
80e8a0b6-001d-423d-b4b2-4522974256dc.raw_text:
MANDAS MARIKANDA
versus
RUTH MAKOMBE
and
ZIMBABWE WOMEN’S MICROFINANCE BANK LIMITED
and
ABRAHAM K MAGUCHU
HIGH COURT OF ZIMBABWE
MAMBARA J
HARARE; 12 and 17 March 2026
Urgent Chamber Application
A Zvarevashe with him T Zintu, for the applicant
D. Mundiya, for the respondents
MAMBARA J:
 [1] This is an urgent chamber application for an interim interdict, brought in terms of rule 60 of the High Court Rules, 2021, in which the applicant seeks an order stopping a disciplinary hearing pending the determination of a declaratory application lodged under case number HCH1145/26. 
[2] The applicant’s final relief, as framed, is that the respondents “be and are hereby interdicted from proceeding with the disciplinary hearing against the applicant pending the determination” of HCH1145/26, with costs against the first respondent. The interim relief mirrors that final relief in substance. 
[3] The application is triggered by a notice of suspension and notice of disciplinary hearing issued on 4 March 2026, placing the applicant on suspension with pay and benefits and convening a disciplinary hearing for 11 March 2026. The disciplinary authority or hearing officer is cited as Mr Abraham K. Maguchu, who is the third respondent in these proceedings. 
[4] The applicant’s case is anchored on an earlier magistrates’ court interdict granted on 1 August 2025, which required the first respondent to “observe corporate governance principles and stop interfering with” the applicant’s “scope of work.” 
[5] In the urgent chamber application, the applicant contends that (i) she has an extant court order barring the first respondent from interference, (ii) notwithstanding that order, the first respondent sought to suspend her and to press forward with disciplinary proceedings, and (iii) unless this court intervenes urgently, the pending disciplinary process will render her pending declaratory proceedings academic. 
The factual setting giving rise to the dispute
[6] It is common cause from the papers that the relationship between the applicant and the second respondent is one of employment, the applicant being employed as Chief Executive Officer of the second respondent. 
[7] The first respondent, who is chairperson of the board of directors of the second respondent, deposes to the opposing affidavit in her personal capacity and on behalf of the second respondent, contending, among other points, that the magistrates’ court order does not prohibit lawful disciplinary action, and that the disciplinary hearing was expressly authorised by a board resolution. 
[8] The first respondent further asserts that she had not been served with the earlier magistrates’ court proceedings or the court order and that she only saw the order for the first time when served with the present urgent chamber application, and she indicates that steps are being taken to seek rescission of that order. 
[9] A further feature of the record is that the board itself resolved (through its non-executive directors) to oppose the litigation in both HCH1145/26 and HCH1146/26, and authorised the first respondent, in her capacity as board chair, to represent the second respondent in the litigation. 
[10] The applicant’s narrative (in the declaratory application) also places emphasis on the history of friction between herself and the first respondent, allegedly dating back to competition for the position of Chief Executive Officer and escalating to the obtaining of the magistrates’ court interdict. 
The point in limine and the parties’ oral submissions
[11] At the commencement of the hearing, Mr Mundiya, for the first and second respondents, raised a point in limine on jurisdiction, and I allowed him to address the court first. 
[12] The respondents’ submission was direct: the matter is a labour dispute between an employer and an employee; it concerns suspension and an impending disciplinary hearing; and, accordingly, it falls within the exclusive province of the labour dispute resolution framework, in particular the Labour Court’s first-instance jurisdiction as settled by the Supreme Court. Counsel relied on the Supreme Court decision in TN Harlequin v Matsvimbo SCB 84/22 and on the Labour Act [Chapter 28:01] provisions (s 89(6) read with s 2A(3)), and submitted that the matter ought to be struck off the roll for want of jurisdiction, with costs on a higher scale. 
[13] In response, Mr Zvarevashe, for the applicant, submitted that the present matter is distinguishable from TN Harlequin. He argued that the issue is not whether the applicant committed misconduct or whether she should be charged, but whether—given the extant civil court judgment barring interference with the applicant’s work—the very person interdicted is permitted to interfere with the applicant’s work “up to being the person who suspends and charges her.” Counsel submitted that this is not a purely labour matter but one founded on the enforcement and protection of rights flowing from the magistrates’ court order, and that the urgent interdict is sought to safeguard a declaratory application pending before this court. He further submitted that one cannot lodge an application in the High Court and then seek protective interim relief from the Labour Court, contending that this court has inherent jurisdiction to entertain the matter. 
[14] In reply, Mr Mundiya submitted that the applicant’s attempt to characterise the matter as not labour-related contradicts the substance of the interim and final relief sought, both of which are tailored to interdict disciplinary proceedings. Counsel added that the first respondent acted as an authorised representative of the employer (the second respondent) and not in her personal capacity, and reiterated that the first respondent was unaware of the magistrates’ court order until it was attached to the present application. 
[15] The crisp question for determination is therefore whether this court has jurisdiction in the first instance to grant interim relief stopping an employer’s disciplinary proceedings against an employee, where the employee seeks to found that relief upon an earlier magistrates’ court interdict against the board chair and has also instituted a declaratory application in this court.
The applicable law on jurisdiction in labour matters
[16] The jurisdictional landscape on the interface between this court’s general jurisdiction and the Labour Court’s specialised jurisdiction is no longer in doubt.
[17] In TN Harlequin Luxaire Ltd v Masvimbo M & 18 Ors 22-SC-084, the Supreme Court considered whether the High Court, purporting to act under s 14 of the High Court Act [Chapter  7:06], could properly issue a declaratur in a matter that was “purely labour in nature,” in light of s 2A(3) of the Labour Act [Chapter 28:01] (Labour Act prevails over any inconsistent enactment) and s 89(6) (no court other than the Labour Court has jurisdiction in the first instance to hear and determine matters referred to in s 89(1)). The court held that the Labour Court has exclusive first-instance jurisdiction in labour matters, and that the High Court erred in assuming jurisdiction in a purely labour dispute. 
[18] The Supreme Court in TN Harlequin further rejected as misconceived the contention (often raised) that the High Court must entertain labour matters because only it can grant declarators. The court observed that the Labour Court routinely issues declaratory-type relief in its daily operations, and emphasised that the existence of s 14 of the High Court Act does not license litigants to bypass the Labour Court where the dispute is, in substance, governed by labour legislation and mechanisms. 
[19] That position has been reaffirmed and applied in the High Court. In Unifreight Africa Ltd v Mukarati N & Anor 25-HH-436, the court declined jurisdiction where the dispute called for the determination of an employment relationship and other employment-related questions, holding that these matters fell within the Labour Court’s exclusive jurisdiction at first instance under ss 2A(3) and 89(6) of the Labour Act, and that the High Court cannot issue a declaratur in respect of labour and employment matters simply because the relief is dressed as a declaration under s 14. 
[20] A further, related principle is that internal disciplinary proceedings (though less formal than courts of law) are nevertheless expected to comply with the requirements of fairness and the audi alteram partem rule. That fairness enquiry is ordinarily undertaken within the labour dispute resolution terrain: first within the disciplinary process itself, and, where appropriate, through the statutory labour remedies. Farm Community Trust v Claudious Chemhere SC 22/13 is authority, among others, for the proposition that disciplinary proceedings cannot, under the guise of flexibility, violate basic fairness with impunity. 
Analysis
[21] The starting point is that jurisdiction is determined by the substance of the dispute and the nature of the relief sought, not by the label that a litigant prefers to attach to the cause.
[22] Here, both the interim and final relief are aimed at one outcome: to stop a disciplinary hearing initiated by the applicant’s employer from proceeding. The immediate juridical incident complained of is the applicant’s suspension and the convening of disciplinary proceedings. 
[23] Suspension from employment (with or without pay) and the conduct of disciplinary proceedings are classic labour and employment incidents regulated by labour law principles and, where applicable, codes of conduct and the Labour Act machinery. They form part of “matters concerning employment” in the ordinary sense in which that term is understood in labour jurisprudence and practice.
[24] It follows that, unless there is some legally cognisable basis upon which the dispute ceases to be one of employment and labour, the matter falls within the exclusive first-instance jurisdiction of the Labour Court, as settled by TN Harlequin Luxaire Ltd v Masvimbo M & 18 Ors 22-SC-084 and applied in Unifreight Africa Ltd v Mukarati N & Anor 25-HH-436.
[25] The applicant’s attempt to relocate the dispute into this court’s jurisdiction rests on the magistrates’ court interdict against the first respondent, barring interference with the applicant’s “scope of work.” 
[26] Even accepting, for present purposes, that the magistrates’ court order is extant and operative (its rescission or otherwise being a separate enquiry), the question remains: does that order transform the present proceedings into a non-labour dispute? In my view it does not.
[27] First, the order—on its face—interdicts the first respondent from “interfering” with the applicant’s scope of work and directs observance of corporate governance principles. It does not expressly purport to immunise the applicant from lawful employment oversight, including disciplinary accountability, nor does it purport to interdict the employer’s statutory and contractual disciplinary processes. 
[28] Second, and more fundamentally, the applicant’s relief is not framed as contempt proceedings to enforce the magistrates’ court order, nor as a narrowly tailored restraint against a defined form of “interference” by the first respondent. Instead, the applicant seeks a wide interdict against the continuation of the disciplinary hearing itself—an employment process—pending the outcome of a High Court declaratory application. 
[29] The gravamen of the dispute therefore remains labour-related: whether disciplinary steps should proceed, and whether they were properly initiated. That is the very type of terrain which TN Harlequin holds the High Court must not enter in the first instance. 
[30] Third, the applicant’s submission that the Labour Court “has no jurisdiction to entertain” declarators or interdicts of the present kind cannot be sustained in the face of TN Harlequin, which expressly puts to rest the notion that the High Court must assume jurisdiction merely because a party seeks declaratory-type relief.
[31] I turn to the aspect that, in the course of oral argument and upon a careful reading of the record, caused disquiet: the first respondent’s prominent role in launching the charges and the suspension, in circumstances where there has been prior confrontation between herself and the applicant that culminated in litigation and a court interdict.
[32] Even on the version advanced by the applicant, the existence of historical tension between the applicant and the first respondent is plain from the papers. In such circumstances, it is undesirable—speaking purely from the standpoint of perception, institutional harmony and the minimisation of allegations of personal vendetta—for disciplinary steps against the applicant to be driven in a manner that places the first respondent “in the lead,” if alternative, less contentious governance arrangements are reasonably available.
[33] That said, concerns about bias, conflict of interest, improper motives, or a misconceived reliance on a prior interdict are matters that can be raised before the disciplinary tribunal itself. The applicant has the right to be heard, to place her position before the hearing officer, and—if the point is well taken—to seek appropriate procedural directions (including, where warranted, challenges framed around fairness).
[34] The disciplinary process is the primary forum to ventilate those concerns because it is the process that is seized with the dispute as an employment incident. If, thereafter, a legally recognised labour grievance arises, it may be pursued through the labour dispute mechanisms, and not by inviting this court to assume first-instance jurisdiction over matters the Labour Court is constitutionally and statutorily designed to handle.
[35] The conclusion I therefore reach is that the present urgent chamber application, whatever its urgency in a practical sense, invites this court to intervene in a disciplinary hearing—a labour matter—in the first instance. That invitation is barred by the now settled jurisprudence.
[36] Having upheld the point in limine on jurisdiction, it is unnecessary, and improper for me to make definitive pronouncements on the merits of the applicant’s asserted right, urgency, or the balance of convenience. The court cannot assume jurisdiction “for a moment” in order to decide those questions.
Disposition
[37] In the result, the point in limine is upheld.
[38] As to costs, the first and second respondents asked for costs on a higher scale, contending that the application is meritless. The applicant resisted that. While I consider the application misconceived for want of jurisdiction, the record shows that the applicant approached this court against the backdrop of an extant magistrates’ court order and an evidently fraught governance relationship between the protagonists. In my view, those features render the approach ill-advised, but not so frivolous as to justify a punitive costs order at this stage.
 	Accordingly, I make the following order:
 The point in limine that this court has no jurisdiction to hear the matter is upheld.
The urgent chamber application is struck off the roll for want of jurisdiction.
 The applicant shall pay the respondents’ costs on the ordinary scale.
mambara J:………………………………
Chivandire Mavhaire and Zinto, applicant’s legal practitioners
Mundiya & mudhara, respondents’ legal practitioners

c7ba2462-5b6a-43b7-87b1-954d6c128fec.filename: DANDIRA v ZIMBABWE POSTS (PRIVATE) LIMITED (ta ZIMPOST) and THE LABOUR COURT JUDGE HONOURABLE MURASI J NO (5733 of 2024) 2026 ZWHHC 89 (11 February 2026).docx
c7ba2462-5b6a-43b7-87b1-954d6c128fec.case_name: DANDIRA v ZIMBABWE POSTS (PRIVATE) LIMITED (ta ZIMPOST) and THE LABOUR COURT JUDGE HONOURABLE MURASI J NO (5733 of 2024) 2026 ZWHHC 89 (11 February 2026).docx
