c7ba2462-5b6a-43b7-87b1-954d6c128fec.raw_text:
SAMSON ZUKWA DANDIRA
versus
ZIMBABWE POSTS (PRIVATE) LIMITED
(t/a ZIMPOST)
and
THE LABOUR COURT JUDGE HONOURABLE MURASI J. NO
HIGH COURT OF ZIMBABWE
MUNANGATI- MANONGWA J
HARARE, May 2025, 11 February 2026
Opposed Matter
Applicant in person
T. T Magwaliba, for the first respondent
           MUNANGATI- MANONGWA J: The applicant approached this court seeking review of the second respondent’s decision sitting at the Labour Court under case number LCH 425/24. The application is brought in terms of rule 62 of the High Court Rules, 2021. This court refused to entertain the application and withheld its jurisdiction until the applicant complied with an extant Labour Court order which required him to pay respondent’s costs before instituting any proceedings that affects first respondent. The applicant has now requested for reasons for such a ruling and hereunder are the reasons.
FACTUAL BACKGROUND
The second respondent made a ruling under case number LCH25/24 whereby applicant’s application was struck off the roll and applicant was ordered to meet respondent’s costs. Again, under case number LCH 425/24 the second respondent issued an order dismissing the applicant’s application for reinstatement LCH25/24, in the process directing applicant to meet respondent’s costs on a legal- practitioner client scale and directing that applicant should not institute any proceedings against respondent on the same matter without paying costs as directed by the order. It should be noted however that the applicant has continued to issue proceedings in this court.
APPLICANT’S CASE
It is the applicant's case that the second respondent improperly struck off the application under case number LCH 25/24 for the adjudication process. This decision was based on the assertion that the applicant failed to motivate his application alleging that the record of proceedings was incomplete as it excluded the supplementary affidavit and the supplementary heads of argument.
Furthermore, the applicant contends that the second respondent unlawfully dismissed the application for reinstatement under case number LCH 425/24, asserting that this dismissal occurred despite the record being incomplete. Specifically, the record did not include the application for leave of the court or the application for the joinder of parties and actions that had yet to be heard and determined. 
The applicant further asserts that he was deprived of the right to have his application for reinstatement heard and determined on its merits. Thus, the applicant sought the review of the judgment by the second respondent. 
RESPONDENT’S CASE
The respondent in its Notice of Opposition identifies two points in limine. First, the respondent asserts that there was non-compliance with the judgment of the Labour Court, LCH 425/24 by the applicant. Specifically, the respondent indicates that the application is defective because the applicant did not adhere to the Labour Court's order, which explicitly prohibited him from instituting any proceedings against the respondent without first paying the respondent's costs.
Second, the respondent contends that the application is, in essence, an appeal disguised as a review. The Labour Court dismissed the applicant's application on its merits, concluding that the dispute between the parties is now res judicata, as it was resolved by the Supreme Court.
It is the respondent's case that the applicant's application for review is without merit. The respondent therefore further asserts that the applicant has not advanced any proper grounds for review that would warrant the attention of this court. The respondent maintains that the Labour Court correctly determined that the applicant failed to provide a reasonable explanation for why his matter was struck off the roll and why it should be reinstated. At the hearing the respondent applied for upliftment of bar for the late filling of the heads of argument. The applicant opposed the application insisting that on several occasions the respondent’s representatives have failed to adhere to court rules and have always sought the court’s indulgence.
ISSUE FOR DETERMINATION
The court had to decide whether it could grant audience to the applicant given his failure to pay costs before instituting another action against respondent. Equally the court had to pronounce on the application for the upliftment of bar by the respondent.
ANALYSIS
The court a quo’s relevant part of the order reads: 
“The application for reinstatement of Case Number LCH25/24, being devoid of merit, is hereby dismissed.
Applicant is to meet Respondent’s costs on a legal practitioner client scale.
Applicant shall not institute any proceedings against the Respondent on the same matter without complying with paragraph 2 above.”
It is imperative to recognize the binding nature of court orders and the legal obligation for compliance by all parties involved. Court orders possess inherent authority, and failure to adhere to such directives constitutes contempt of court. Specifically, contempt arises when an individual willfully and mala fide refuses to obey a standing order issued by the court. This principle underscores the judiciary's role in upholding the rule of law and ensuring that its directives are respected and followed.
In the present instance, it is crucial to emphasize that the order in question which required applicant to pay respondent’s costs had been duly served on the applicant in case number LCHC 425/24, thus the applicant was fully aware of the obligations imposed upon him. Despite this awareness, the applicant chose to deliberately and consciously disregard the court's order and proceeded to institute these proceedings. The case of Wilson v Minister of Defence & Ors [1999] (1) ZLR 144 (HC) exemplifies this point, as it establishes the legal precedent that an individual who knowingly contravenes a court order is in contempt. An extant court order is not set aside by simply defying it. See Madzimbamuto v Musamadiya HH235/24.The applicant contends that the second respondent’s judgment is incorrect and hence the justification for noncompliance with the order. Lord RADDIFFE in Smith v East Elloe Rural District Council [1956] AC 736 at 769 highlighted that: 
“An order, even if not made in good faith, is still an act capable of legal consequences. It bears no brand of illegality on its forehead. Unless the necessary procedures are taken at law to establish the cause of invalidity and to get it quashed or otherwise upset, it will remain as effective for its ostensible purpose as the most impeccable of orders.” 
By proceeding to approach this court whilst ignoring an obligation imposed by a competent court, the applicant showed disdain and disrespect to authority. These courts will not entertain litigants who chose not to obey orders and still seek the court’s services. The applicant is thus approaching the court with dirty hands. Until the applicant purges his defiance, he will not be granted audience. 
As for the application for upliftment of bar the court found that there was negligence on the part of the respondents and a pattern of neglecting to adhere to the rules of court was developing on the respondent’s part as exemplified by requests from conciliation to arbitration, to the Labour Court up to the Supreme Court where, in nearly all the instances the respondent would apply for upliftment of bar. It is not in the interests of justice for the courts to continue to condone such failures more particularly where the 1st respondent is represented. In that regard the application for upliftment of bar is dismissed. In essence therefore should the applicant purge its failure to observe the concerned court order, the matter will get decided without first respondent’s participation due to the operation of the bar.
Therefore, this court issued the following order:
The 1st respondent’s application for the upliftment of bar is dismissed.
The court withholds its jurisdiction until the applicant complies with the order for payment of first respondent’s costs.
There shall be no order as to costs.
Munangati- Manongwa J……………………….....................
Applicant in person
Dube, Manikai and Hwacha, first respondent’s legal practitioners

f07336b3-e34f-47e3-8a88-677480e4aa88.filename: COURTAULD TRUST v  MINISTER OF PUBLIC SERVICE LABOUR AND SOCIAL WELFARE NO   and   THE REGISTER OF DEEDS (7096 of 2025) 2026 ZWHHC 93 (12 February 2026).pdf
f07336b3-e34f-47e3-8a88-677480e4aa88.case_name: COURTAULD TRUST v  MINISTER OF PUBLIC SERVICE LABOUR AND SOCIAL WELFARE NO   and   THE REGISTER OF DEEDS (7096 of 2025) 2026 ZWHHC 93 (12 February 2026).pdf
f07336b3-e34f-47e3-8a88-677480e4aa88.citation: In our law, as espoused in Crundall Bros (Pvt) Ltd v Lazarus NO & Anor 1991(2) ZLR 125(S) at
f07336b3-e34f-47e3-8a88-677480e4aa88.summary: MINISTER OF PUBLIC SERVICE, LABOUR AND SOCIAL WELFARE N.O
f07336b3-e34f-47e3-8a88-677480e4aa88.raw_text:
HH- 138-26
HCH-7096-25
COURTAULD TRUST
versus
MINISTER OF PUBLIC SERVICE, LABOUR AND SOCIAL WELFARE N.O
and
THE REGISTER OF DEEDS
HIGH COURT OF ZIMBABWE
CHIRAWU-MUGOMBA J
Harare 28 January, 11 & 12 February 2026
C. McGown, for the applicant
UNOPPOSED COURT APPLICATION
CHIRAWU-MUGOMBA J: This matter was placed before me on the unopposed motion
roll. Upon perusal, I was prima facie not convinced with the propriety of the application
and the order sought especially in view of the fact that none of the original trustees or
those appointed to replace them were still alive. I removed the matter from the roll and
directed that heads of argument be filed in support of the order sought framed as
follows.
1. The following persons be and are hereby appointed as trustees to the Courtauld
Trust:
Robert Edward Anderson
William Archibald Graham Black
Jonathan Richard Waters
1

Muchadeyi Ashton Masunda
Evelyn Joan Uzelac
2. There shall be no order as to costs
The facts of the matter are as follows. The applicant was registered with the
first respondent in the year 1969 under Registration Number 20/69. On the 10th of
January 1964, a deed of donation and trust was executed in which Viginia Courtauld as
the ‘donor’ established the applicant and gave and endowed it with the gift of 40 000
pounds for the establishment of a farm training centre. The donor amended the deed in
1965 and 1967. The original appointed trustees were Stephen Lewis Courtauld, Virginia
Courtauld, Honourable Tom Ian Findlay Wilson, Jacqueline Wilson and Percy Gordon
Deedes. One Brian Woodall Skene O’Connell was added as a trustee.
Various changes occurred including the death of Sir Stephen Courtauld and as at
the date of the signing of the last notarial deed as at the 28th of October 1967, the
trustees were the following- Phyllis Edith Gallagher as the authorised agent of Virginia
Courtauld, Tom Ian Findlay Wilson, Jacqueline Wilson, Brian Woodall Skene O’Connell
and John Winter Hodges. With the passage of time, trustees passed away and new
ones were added. The last minutes of a meeting of the applicant that occurred in 1999
on the 17th of November 1999 indicate that the trustees were the following- R.R.J
Putterill as the chair, R.E Park, A.LA. Pichanick. J.W Welsh and A.H.G Hampshire. All
these persons have passed on. There is a dearth of information since the applicant has
not been operating from that time. Since then a group of concerned people have come
together with a view to resuscitating the applicant. They have all willing to be appointed
as trustees. These are the names that appear in the draft order.
At the hearing, I posed the question as to whether the applicant and those that he
represents have locus standi and the authority of this court to appoint trustees in the
absence of a provision in the trust deed granting such authority. The following
submissions were made. In Trustee Mahommedan Mosque Sayed, 1937 NPD, it was
held that the court does have requisite jurisdiction to appoint trustees to hold certain
property. In Trustees of the Leonard Cheshire Homes Zimbabwe Limited vs. Chiite and
2

ors, HH-267-10, the court considered the issue of the locus standi as except one of
them, the trustees’ term of office had expired or they had exceeded their terms. The
court held that a trust does not fail due to absence of trustees. The court considered
also the implications of the Companies and Associations Trustees Act, [Chapter 24:04].
Further it was submitted that the cy-pres doctrine does not apply since the trust has not
failed.
It is common cause that the trust deed for applicant did not envisage a situation
where all trustees would have passed on. In my view, the questions or issues to be
addressed are the following:
1. What happens to a trust set up through a trust deed when all trustees have
passed away and the deed has no provision for successors?
2. Who should in such circumstances make an application for the revival of the
trust and how should this be done?
Under South African law, when all trustees pass away and the trust deed is silent
on succession, the trust itself does not die. The Trust Property Control Act 57 of 1988
deals with this scenario in s7(1) as follows:
7. Appointment of trustee and co-trustee by Master
(1)If the office of trustee cannot be filled or becomes vacant, the Master shall, in the absence of
any provision in the trust instrument, after consultation with so many interested parties as he
may deem necessary, appoint any person as trustee.(2)When the Master considers it desirable,
he may, notwithstanding the provisions of the trust instrument, appoint as co-trustee of any
serving trustee any person whom he deems fit.
Interested parties can be beneficiaries, executor of the last deceased’s trustee’s
estate or even the state. South African law is also underpinned by the legal notion that
trust assets do not form part of the deceased trustees personal estate- see ESTATE
Kemp vs MacDonald’s Trustees , 1915 AD 891.
While the section itself deals with the appointment through the Master, the High
Court still retains inherent jurisdiction to appoint trustees.
I inquired from Mr McGown whether or not he is aware of the cy-pres doctrine. In
the heads of argument, he submitted that this doctrine is not applicable to this case.
The doctrine was described in ex parte Hart 1947 (4) SA 464 (W) as follows.
3

“ It is settled law that a fund given or bequeathed for one charitable purpose may, on the
accomplishment of failure of that purpose, be devoted, with the leave of the court, to an
analogous purpose. Marks v Estate Gluckman (1956 ad at p. 305). In English law, this is known
as the cy-pres doctrine and in cases in our courts in which it has been sought to apply the above
doctrine reference has been made to decisions in the English courts”
Mr McGown also submitted that the Companies and Trustees Associations Act
[Chapter 24:04 ] does not apply. In terms of that act, an association is described as,
In casu, no sufficient information has been placed before the court to determine
whether or not the act applies . The major advantage of the act is that in section 7, the
High Court is given the mandate to appoint trustees upon application in situations of
death or other circumstances. This would have been the perfect act applicable had it
been shown that the applicant falls within the definition of an association. The court
may even regulate future appointments in terms of s8. This would work well for the
applicant.
In Re Malilangwe Trust, SC-13-22, KUDYA JA, had occasion to deal with issues
relating to trusts as follows,
In our law, as espoused in Crundall Bros (Pvt) Ltd v Lazarus NO & Anor 1991(2) ZLR 125(S) at
128F, a “trust is not a person”. It must be represented by a trustee or trustees, nomine officii.
This position is affirmed in the Veritas case, supra para [20] and Chiite & 7 Others v Trustees,
Leonard Cheshire Homes Zimbabwe Central Trust CCZ 10/17. These authorities and in particular
the Veritas case, supra, also lay down the principle that the founding affidavit of a trust suing in
its name must be deposed to by an authorized trustee and not by an employee of the trustee/s
and not in the trade name of the trust to satisfy the requirement that it is the “trust” that is before
the court. I am aware that this Court in the recent judgment of Sharadkumah Patel & Anor v The
Cosmo Trust & Ors SC 165/21 at para [44] held that a trust has the legal capacity to sue in its
own name or in the name of a trustee. That finding does not appear to affect the principle raised
in the above cited cases that the founding affidavit of an application of such a trust must be
deposed to by a trustee to render the application valid.
In casu, as has been shown, we have a peculiar situation in which the original
trustees and all those that replaced them are deceased. Who then should apply for the
appointment of new trustees? While I agree generally that the High Court retains the
common law right to appoint trustees and that a trust does not die upon death of all the
4

trustees and there is no succession clause in the trust deed, in my view, the person or
persons who petition the court must be interested persons. The applicant and those
that seek appointment call themselves, ‘nominal’ trustees and they even state in the
resolution of the 15th of January 2025 that it is one by the applicant. The question then
becomes this? Who nominated them? Where are they coming from? What is their
interest given the fact that the applicant has not been operational for a long time? The
time period between the last minutes of the properly constituted board of trustees and
the resolution is twenty-six years. All that has been presented are some curriculum
vitaes with no link whatsoever to the applicant. No information has even been
presented as to what is happening to the various activities. If one has regard to the
minutes of the properly constituted board of trustees, it is apparent that applicant was
involved in many activities. It seems that the deponent and those that appear in the
draft order ‘constituted’ themselves from literally nowhere and have given the deponent
‘powers’ even to deal with the first respondent. That is not acceptable. They have no
locus standi at all to act on behalf of the applicant.
DISPOSITION
I find no merit in the application on the basis that the deponent to the affidavit and
those that he purports to represent have not shown that they are interested parties.
They have no locus standi. On costs, it is trite that these are at the discretion of the
court and as prayed for in the draft order, there shall be no order as to costs.
1. The application be and is hereby dismissed.
2. There shall be no order as to costs.
Warhurst Attorneys- Applicant’s legal practitioners
5

6

94e5495a-c498-44e8-853b-ae76d94dc8ce.filename: Mucheche v Minister Of Public Service Labour And Social Welfare NO And Registrar Of Labour NO (HCH 619625) 2026 ZWHHC 422 (20 April 2026).docx
94e5495a-c498-44e8-853b-ae76d94dc8ce.case_name: Mucheche v Minister Of Public Service Labour And Social Welfare NO And Registrar Of Labour NO (HCH 619625) 2026 ZWHHC 422 (20 April 2026).docx
94e5495a-c498-44e8-853b-ae76d94dc8ce.citation: Per contra, Mr Mucheche argued that the word “together” simply means “simultaneously”, “pari passu”, “contemporaneously”, or “at the same time”. He argued that that is the simple, ordinary grammatical meaning. The court adopts the ordinary grammatical meaning of the words used by the legislature. The point of law was brought to the respondents' attention by the letter uploaded on 16 December 2025, which appears at p 18 of the record. It was incumbent upon the respondents to do the needful, which they did not. He further submitted that the issue is a question of law which goes to the root of the proceedings. He cited the case of Muchakata v Netherburn Mine 1996 (1) ZLR 153 (S), which held that a question of law can be raised at any time if it goes to the root of the proceedings. This issue goes to the root of the proceedings. In the interest of fair play, the issue was brought to the respondents' attention by letter, as a matter of courtesy. The respondents completely ignored this issue. They downplayed it. It is a critical issue because it affects the integrity of the integrated electronic case management system.
subject_chains: “I should add that whether or not South Africa did or did not fail to do so is a question of fact upon which there was no evidence before the court a quo, and for reasons too obvious to require enumeration, the learned Judge was not entitled to enquire into this issue of fact after reserving judgment and without any reference to the parties, and then to decide it. Compare Kauesa v Minister of Home Affairs and Others 1996 (4) SA 965 (NmS) at 973H – 974C.”

“It is trite that where the word ‘shall’ is used it denotes a mandatory requirement.  Thus, the use of the word “shall” in s 3 (1) (c) is imperative – it denotes that the section is peremptory, and thus compliance with its provisions is mandatory.”
94e5495a-c498-44e8-853b-ae76d94dc8ce.summary: MINISTER OF PUBLIC SERVICE, LABOUR AND SOCIAL WELFARE N.O.
94e5495a-c498-44e8-853b-ae76d94dc8ce.raw_text:
CALEB MUCHECHE 
versus
MINISTER OF PUBLIC SERVICE, LABOUR AND SOCIAL WELFARE N.O. 
and
REGISTRAR OF LABOUR N.O.
HIGH COURT OF ZIMBABWE
DEMBURE J
HARARE, 27 March & 20 April 2026
Court Application
Applicant in person 
T J Gundumura, for the respondents 
DEMBURE J:	
On 27 March 2026, the court, after hearing submissions from the applicant and counsel for the respondents, handed down an ex tempore judgment in terms of which I found the respondents’ opposition to be fatally defective. The respondents’ opposing papers were accordingly struck out, and the matter was dealt with as unopposed. I thereafter granted the application in terms of the draft order, and the terms of the said order read as follows:
“1. 	The instant application be and is hereby granted.
2. 	It is hereby declared that the systematic failure by the 1st and 2nd Respondents to ensure that all Employment Codes of Conduct under section 101 of the Labour Act [Chapter 28:01], National Code of Conduct SI 15 of 2006, Public Service Regulations SI 1 of 2000 as amended and any other employment/workplace code of conduct like the Health Services Regulations, Judicial Service Regulations, Military Service Regulations, Prisons and Correctional Services Regulations, State Security Staff code of conduct/regulations are translated into all the constitutionally recognised official languages is in fragrant violation of sections 2, 6, 56, 63 and 65 of the Constitution of Zimbabwe.
3. 	The Registrar of Labour be and is hereby directed to ensure and enforce the mandatory translation of existing and newly registered Employment Codes of Conduct from English language into all the constitutionally recognised official languages of Zimbabwe within three (3) months of the granting of the order.
4. 	The 1st Respondent shall, within three (3) months of the granting of this order, initiate and/or complete the necessary legislative amendments to the Labour Relations (Employment Code of Conduct) Regulations, 1990 (SI 379 of 1990) to explicitly and fully incorporate all officially recognised languages of Zimbabwe, as provided for by section 6 of the Constitution of Zimbabwe.
5. 	There is no order as to costs.”
	The respondents’ legal practitioners requested the written reasons for the court’s decision. The following are the full written reasons thereof:
FACTUAL CONSPECTUS
The applicant is Caleb Mucheche, an adult male Zimbabwean. He pleaded that he is the first President of the Zimbabwe African Labour Law Society/Association for Legal Practitioners in Zimbabwe, the founder President of the Labour Association Zimbabwe, a legal author/researcher/lawyer, and a law lecturer. He is also the former Dean of the Law Faculty of a Zimbabwean University. He averred that he felt compelled by conscience and a sense of legal justice to bring this court application as pro-bono public interest litigation to challenge the disenfranchisement perpetrated against the majority of Zimbabwean workers. The first respondent is the Minister of Public Service, Labour and Social Welfare, cited in his official capacity as the state official responsible for overseeing the entire labour regulatory framework. The second respondent is the Registrar of Labour, also cited in his/her official capacity as the state official responsible for the registration, oversight and enforcement of employment codes of conduct and the Labour Relations (Employment Code of Conduct) Regulations, 1990 (SI 379 of 1990).
The applicant contended that the respondents’ failure to ensure that registered employment codes of conduct are accessible in all official languages recognised under s 6(1) of the Constitution was unlawful and a denial of fundamental human rights. He asserted that the first respondent is the regulatory authority who, under s 17 of the Labour Act [Chapter 28:01] (“the Act”), is obligated to promulgate necessary regulations to operationalise the provisions of the Act. This constitutional obligation is to ensure that any statutory instrument is consonant with the fundamental principles enshrined in the Constitution. It was further averred that s 6(1) of the Constitution grants equal legal status to all official languages of Zimbabwe, which include, inter alia, English, Chewa, Kalanga, Nambiya, Ndebele, Shona, Sotho, Tonga, Venda, and sign language. 
It was argued that the failure to ensure that the employment codes of conduct are translated into all the official languages violates the constitutional right of workers to equality and equal benefit and protection of the law as enshrined in s 56(1) of the Constitution. The same violates the right to fair labour practices and fair labour standards enshrined under s 65. His position was that the codes of conduct must be accessible in other official languages, and that their availability only in English infringes on workers’ fundamental rights. The applicant also related to s 63(1) of the Constitution. He further asserted that, under such circumstances, the workplace disciplinary processes prejudice workers, as their codes are not available in their local languages. The applicant accordingly sought a declaratory order and consequential relief directing the respondents to ensure the mandatory translation of all registered employment codes of conduct, the national code of conduct SI 15 of 2006, and any other employment codes of conduct into the constitutionally recognised official languages in terms of s 6 of the Constitution, within the stipulated period.
It is common cause that on 15 December 2025, the first and second respondents, through their legal practitioners, filed their Notice of Opposition. The applicant filed a letter to the Registrar on 16 December 2025, stating that no opposing affidavit was filed with the Notice of Opposition on 15 December 2025. It was further stated that the application ought to be treated as unopposed since the dies induciae had lapsed by operation of law. On 16 December 2025, the first and second respondents filed their opposing affidavit. In the said opposing affidavit, points in limine were raised that:
1.	The applicant lacked the locus standi to institute the present proceedings as provided for under s 85 of the Constitution;
2.	There was improper use of language and scandalous averments in the founding affidavit; and
3.	There was a fatal failure to cite the relevant parties, namely the Attorney-General of Zimbabwe and the affected employer or workers’ organisations.
On the merits, it was contended that the application lacked merit. The respondents denied having any legal obligation to translate documents or employment codes of conduct. They averred that they do not author them as they are authored by employment councils or workers councils. They denied that equality before the law is measured by language and accessibility. It was further contended that the applicant was being theoretical and invited the court to decide an abstract dispute. That employers explain codes of conduct to their employees during disciplinary processes, and that it was incorrect to argue that the processes are unfair simply because the codes are in the English language only. It was denied that the Minister, by failing to translate the employment codes, is in breach of his constitutional duty. It was also pointed out that the application lacked substance and ought to be dismissed. 
In his answering affidavit, the applicant contended that the points in limine were devoid of merit and insisted on the application.
When the hearing commenced, the parties made submissions on the points in limine raised by the respondents. As I was in the process of delivering my decision, and before I could pronounce the decision on the points in limine, it dawned on me that there was an anomaly regarding the respondents’ papers. I paused and immediately raised the point of law. The point of law related to the filing of the respondents’ notice of opposition separately from the opposing affidavit. The legal issue was whether the respondents had complied with rule 59(7) of the High Court Rules, 2021, given the manner in which they filed their papers: a notice of opposition on 15 December 2025, while their opposing affidavit was filed separately on 16 December 2025. I invited the parties to make submissions on the point, and the following submissions were made. 
THE ORAL SUBMISSIONS ON THE POINT OF LAW
	Mr Gundumura submitted that both the notice of opposition and the opposing affidavits were filed right on time. He argued that while they appear to have different dates by the Registrar, the 15th for the notice and the opposing affidavit, the 16th, the papers were properly before the court. The notice and opposing affidavit were filed within the timeframe stipulated by the rules. He insisted that they were filed together. When the court queried how it could be argued that the papers were filed together, given the two different dates, counsel contended that on the face of it, that would appear as if they were not filed together, but if we look at it purposively, we might come to a different conclusion due to the fact that they were filed within the 10-day period. 
	Even when the court referred him to r 59(7), counsel insisted that the documents were filed together. When I further referred counsel to the provisions of SI 81/2024 on what the term “date of filing” means, he remained adamant that this is the actual date upon which the documents in question were actioned by the Registrar. He further submitted that there were no objections by the applicant in the heads of argument or answering affidavit to the effect that these documents were not filed properly or filed out of time. He, however, admitted that the letter at p 18 of the record raised the issue. I hasten to state that it appeared to me that counsel did not understand the point of law I raised and the meaning of rule 59(7). He even questioned whether they had to refile the notice of opposition and the opposing affidavit. I could not give him legal advice, unfortunately. 
	Per contra, Mr Mucheche argued that the word “together” simply means “simultaneously”, “pari passu”, “contemporaneously”, or “at the same time”. He argued that that is the simple, ordinary grammatical meaning. The court adopts the ordinary grammatical meaning of the words used by the legislature. The point of law was brought to the respondents' attention by the letter uploaded on 16 December 2025, which appears at p 18 of the record. It was incumbent upon the respondents to do the needful, which they did not. He further submitted that the issue is a question of law which goes to the root of the proceedings. He cited the case of Muchakata v Netherburn Mine 1996 (1) ZLR 153 (S), which held that a question of law can be raised at any time if it goes to the root of the proceedings. This issue goes to the root of the proceedings. In the interest of fair play, the issue was brought to the respondents' attention by letter, as a matter of courtesy. The respondents completely ignored this issue. They downplayed it. It is a critical issue because it affects the integrity of the integrated electronic case management system.
Counsel went on to submit that the date the Registrar stamps the document is the official, authentic date that must be recognised. In this case, it was a piecemeal filing of documents, or filing in instalments. The rules do not allow that. The issue non-suited the respondents. Respondents who entered via the back door do not have to remain. Once you are picked to have entered via the back door, you get crushed. Then the proper course of action is that you have to leave and follow the front door. The rules must be obeyed, and the same applies to the Attorney General's office. If the court's view is that the respondents ought to be given that latitude or indulgence, it also equally applies that they cannot cling to their perceived preliminary points in light of the fact that perhaps it is typical of a party trying to call a kettle black. 
In reply, Mr Gundumura argued that the procedural irregularity can be condoned on the basis that, first, the notice of opposition was filed on time, and that the opposing affidavits were filed within the 10-day period as required by the rules. What was outstanding, which the respondents were now seeking, was the court's condonation of the respondents’ failure to have the notice of opposition and the opposing affidavits consolidated into one document. But generally, these two documents were filed on time. The applicant did not pursue or argue that these two documents were filed out of time, and they are not causing any prejudice to him in the present circumstances. He submitted that the court could condone the respondents so that we could proceed under such circumstances. 
Given that Mr Gundumura related to condonation, which aspect Mr Mucheche had not fully addressed, I gave him the opportunity to address me after the address by the respondents’ counsel. Mr Mucheche argued that a legal nullity cannot be condoned. Once the notice and the opposing affidavit are filed separately, those documents bear a different legal complexion altogether. Rule 59(7) is mandatory. The issue was raised in the letter at p 18 and not addressed. There is, therefore, no condonation to be applied for in the face of a nullity. The Macfoy v United Africa Co. Ltd [1961] 3 All ER 1169 case demonstrates that a nullity cannot be condoned. 
He further submitted that under the circumstances, the belated application for condonation was misplaced. It is like placing the cart before the horse. It has no legal basis or substance. The respondents were supposed to withdraw the defective documents and then file an application for condonation so that a proper notice of opposition would be filed. Under the circumstances, the respondents cannot, on this particular point, apply for condonation. You cannot apply for condonation for what is not there. A legal nullity cannot be condoned. The issue of having a second bite of the cherry is a prejudice on its own. It was also submitted that no application for condonation had been made before the court. That there was nothing to be condoned. The application must be treated as unopposed. 
THE PRELIMINARY QUESTION FOR DETERMINATION
  	I must first determine whether or not the respondents’ opposing papers are valid and properly before the court.
APPLICATION OF THE LAW TO THE FACTS
It is trite that a court of law must be satisfied that it has valid pleadings before it or that the parties are properly before it.  It is also an established principle of law that the court is at liberty to raise a point of law which goes to the root of the proceedings or that affects the validity of the proceedings before it at any stage of the proceedings. Once it has raised a point of law mero motu it is obliged by law to hear the parties in submissions before it can determine the issue. This position was affirmed in Nzara & Ors v Kashumba N.O. & Ors SC 18/18 at pp 11-12 where UCHENA JA said:
“The function of a court is to determine disputes placed before it by the parties. It cannot go on a frolic of its own. Where a point of law or a factual issue exercises the court’s mind but has not been raised by the parties or addressed by them either in their pleadings in evidence or in submissions from the bar, the court is at liberty to put the question to the parties and ask them to make submissions on the matter. 
In Welkom Municipality v Masureik and Herman T/A Lotus Corp 1997 (3) SA 363 at 371 G-H Marais JA commenting on what the court should base its decision on said:
“I should add that whether or not South Africa did or did not fail to do so is a question of fact upon which there was no evidence before the court a quo, and for reasons too obvious to require enumeration, the learned Judge was not entitled to enquire into this issue of fact after reserving judgment and without any reference to the parties, and then to decide it. Compare Kauesa v Minister of Home Affairs and Others 1996 (4) SA 965 (NmS) at 973H – 974C.” 
In the Namibian case of Kauesa v Minister of Home Affairs and Others 1996 (4) 965 (NmS) DUMBUTSHENA AJA at pages 973H to 974C said:
“The above matters are not crucial to the determination of this appeal. They are however, important because a frequent departure from counsel’s, more correctly the litigant’s case, may be wrongly interpreted by those who seek justice in our courts of law. It is the litigants who must be heard and not a judicial officer.
It would be wrong for judicial officers to rely for their decisions on matters not put before them by litigants either in evidence or in oral or written submissions. Now and again, a Judge comes across a point not argued before him by counsel but which he thinks material to the resolution of the case. It is his duty in such a circumstance to inform counsel on both sides and invite them to submit arguments either for or against the Judge’s point. It is undesirable for a court to deliver a judgment with a substantial portion containing issues never canvassed or relied on by counsel...” (my emphasis)
In any case, a point of law that goes to the root of the proceedings can be raised at any time, even by the court mero motu. See also Muchakata v Netherburn Mine (supra).
	The applicable rule outlining how the notice of opposition and the opposing affidavit(s) must be filed is r 59(7). It provides as follows:
“(7) 	The respondent shall be entitled, within the time given in the court application in accordance with subrule (6), to file a notice of opposition, together with one or more opposing affidavits.” (my emphasis)
By the use of the word “shall”, the provisions are clearly peremptory. The law is settled that where the word “shall” is used in a statute as opposed to the word “may”, it shows that the legislature, in this case, the drafters of the rules, intended to make compliance mandatory or peremptory. Thus, in Takashinga Kare Housing Pay Scheme v City of Harare & Ors SC 88/25 at pp 12-13, MWAYERA JA restated the law in the following words:
“29. … It is an established principle that the use of the word “shall” in a statutory provision denotes the legislature’s intention of imposing a mandatory or peremptory obligation as opposed to the use of the word "may," which typically suggests a permissive or discretionary provision.
30. In Mukwaira v Minister of Lands, Agriculture, Fisheries, Water & Rural Resettlement                         SC 15/24, at p 15, the following pertinent sentiments were made:
“It is trite that where the word ‘shall’ is used it denotes a mandatory requirement.  Thus, the use of the word “shall” in s 3 (1) (c) is imperative – it denotes that the section is peremptory, and thus compliance with its provisions is mandatory.”
Similarly, in Shumba & Anor v ZEC & Anor 2008 (2) ZLR 65 (S) at 80 D-G, this Court held: 
“It is the generally accepted rule of interpretation that the use of peremptory words such as “shall” as opposed to “may” is indicative of the legislature’s intention to make the provision peremptory. The use of the word “may” as opposed to “shall” is construed as indicative of the legislature’s intention to make a provision directory. In some instances, the legislature explicitly provides that failure to comply with a statutory provision is fatal. In other instances, the legislature specifically provides that failure to comply is not fatal. In both of the above instances no difficulty arises. The difficulty usually arises where the legislature has made no specific indication as to whether failure to comply is fatal or not.” 
Further, in Sibanda & Anor v Ncube & Ors/ Khumalo & Anor v Mudimba & Ors SC 158-20 at p. 15, Patel JA (as he then was) held that: 
“The broad test for ascertaining the true nature of a statutory duty was enunciated more than a century ago in the case of Howard v Bodington (1877) 2 PD 203, at 211: ‘… in each case you must look to the subject-matter, consider the importance of the provision and the relation of that provision to the general object intended to be secured by the Act, and upon a review of the case on that aspect decide whether the enactment is what is called imperative or only directory…’”
In Chirosva Minerals (Pvt) Ltd v Minister of Mines and Ors 2011 (2) ZLR 274, the court held that the disregard of a peremptory provision in a statute is fatal to the validity of the proceedings affected.” (my emphasis)
	The law is clear, therefore, that non-compliance with a peremptory provision of a statute renders the proceedings or any process thereof a nullity. The same position was confirmed in Moyo & Ors v Zvoma & Anor SC 28/10 at p 14. 
There is no doubt that rule 59(7) is peremptory, and any non-compliance therewith renders the purported opposing papers fatally defective. What is critical in that rule is that the notice of opposition and the opposing affidavit ought to be filed together. The cardinal rule of interpretation is that where the language used in a statute is clear and unambiguous, the words must be given the ordinary grammatical meaning unless to do so would lead to an absurdity. See Zambezi Gas Zimbabwe v N R Barber (Pvt) Ltd & Anor 2020 (1) ZLR 138 (S) at 143. The word “together” must be interpreted in its ordinary grammatical meaning, and there is no ambiguity that arises in the context of the peremptory provision.  According to the Oxford Learner’s Dictionary, the word “together” means: “with something else”, “at the same time”, “simultaneously” or “combined/joined”. This means that to constitute a filing together, the notice of opposition must be filed at the same time or combined with the opposing affidavit(s). None can validly stand on its own in the absence of the other. 
In this case, it is apparent from pages 16 and 19 that the notice of opposition was filed on 15 December 2025, while the opposing affidavit was then filed or accepted by the Registrar on 16 December 2025 as a stand-alone document. The date of filing is clearly defined in s 3 of SI 81/2024, which amended the current High Court Rules, 2021, as follows:
“date of filing” means the date on which any pleading envisaged by these rules is lodged with and accepted by the Registrar.” (my emphasis)
	There is no doubt, in this case, that the date upon which the notice of opposition and the opposing affidavit were accepted by the Registrar is different. The documents were filed separately and on different dates. The Registrar's endorsements that the document was accepted show the date of filing. To that extent, I found it quite strange that Mr Gundumura submitted that these documents were filed together or at the same time. There ought to have been no argument, clearly, as the notice of opposition was duly stamped 15 December 2025, while the opposing affidavit was stamped 16 December 2025. The documents were filed separately and not together as per the mandatory requirement of rule 59(7). There was no point in arguing that the sets of documents were filed together. The respondents failed to comply with the peremptory provisions of r 59(7). This rendered the respondents’ purported opposing papers fatally defective and, therefore, a nullity. 
When my exchanges with counsel for the respondents continued, he belatedly sought to argue that this irregularity could be condoned on the basis that the papers were, in any case, filed within the dies induciae and that there would be no prejudice to the applicant. The attitude taken by counsel was simply that condonation is there for the mere asking. It is trite that the indulgence of condonation can only be granted provided that the applicant has made a proper application and has satisfied the basic requirements for an application of that nature namely that the delay was inordinate in the circumstances, the explanation for the delay was reasonable, that there are prospects of success on the merits and that there is no possibility of prejudice to the respondent if the application is granted. See Doves Funeral Assurance (Private) Limited v Harare Motorway (Private) Limited and 4 Others SC 63/23 at pp 6-7. 
However, in this case, the effect of non-compliance with the peremptory provisions of r 59(7) rendered the respondents’ opposition fatally defective or a nullity. It is trite that a nullity cannot be condoned or amended. It is nothing upon which nothing can stand. This legal position was long settled in Jensen v Acavalos 1993 (1) ZLR 216 (S). The judgment was extensively quoted in Moyo & Ors v Zvoma & Anor from pp 12-14, where CHIDYAUSIKU CJ (as he then was) held as follows:
“In Jensen v Acavalos 1993 (1) ZLR 216 this Court held that by use of the word "shall" compliance with the requirement of r 29 was peremptory and that failure to comply with the rule rendered the Notice of Appeal a nullity and that such a notice cannot be condoned or amended.   KORSAH JA at 219D had this to say about the Notice of Appeal that did not comply with r 29 of the Supreme Court Rules:
"This notice of appeal was defective for non-compliance with the mandatory provisions of Rule 29, subrules (c), (d) and (e) which require the applicant or his legal representative to state: (i) whether the whole or only part of the judgment is appealed against; (ii) the ground of appeal to be set forth concisely and in separately numbered paragraphs; and (iii) the exact nature of the relief which is sought."
The learned JUDGE OF APPEAL further stated at pp 219H-220D:
"The notice of appeal, being bad for non-compliance with the rules, was not cured by the filing on 3 January 1990, of grounds of appeal without a prayer. Indeed, even if the grounds of appeal filed on 3 January 1990 had contained a prayer for relief, it would not have been effectual in validating the defective notice of appeal.
The reason is that a notice of appeal which does not comply with the rules is fatally defective and invalid.   That is to say, it is a nullity.   It is not only bad but incurably bad, and, unless the court is prepared to grant an application for condonation of the defect and to allow a proper notice of appeal to be filed, the appeal must be struck off the roll with costs:  De Jager v Diner & Anor 1957 (3) SA 567 (A) at 574 C-D.
In Hattingh v Pienaar 1977 (2) SA 182 (O) … at 183, KLOPPER JP held that a fatally defective compliance with the rules regarding the filing of appeals cannot be condoned or amended. What should actually be applied for is an extension of time within which to comply with the relevant rule. With this view I most respectfully agree; for if the notice of appeal is incurably bad, then, to borrow the words of LORD DENNING in McFoy v United Africa Co Ltd [1961] 3 All ER 1169 (PC) at 1172I, 'every proceeding which is founded on it is also bad and incurably bad.   You cannot put something on nothing and expect it to stay there. It will collapse'."
GUBBAY CJ and MANYARARA JA concurred. In Matanhire v BP & Shell Marketing Services (Pvt) Ltd 2004 (2) ZLR 147 (S) MALABA JA (as he then was) expressed the same sentiments when he stated at 149 E-G:
"A nullity cannot be amended.   In Jensen v Acavalos 1993 (1) ZLR 216 (S) KORSAH JA at 220B said that the reason why a fatally defective notice of appeal could not be amended was that:
'… it is not only bad but incurably bad'.
Citing Hattingh v Pienaar 1977 (2) SA 182 (O) at 183 for authority, the learned JUDGE OF APPEAL said that what should actually be applied for is an extension of time within which to comply with the relevant rule and condonation of non-compliance.
…
It is quite clear from the above authorities that failure to comply with peremptory language of a Statute can lead to a nullity.” (my emphasis)
The respondents’ papers lacked legal existence. They were a legal nullity. Counsel could not ask me to condone a nullity or accept those papers. A legal nullity begets no valid process. It is incurably bad, and nothing can flow from a nullity. The papers could not be accepted as they did not exist at law. The respondents could only properly apply for condonation and extension of time to file a proper notice of opposition and opposing affidavit. He could not seek that the court condone a nullity. 
Mr Gundumura adopted a cavalier approach. Initially, he stood his ground, insisting that the papers were valid. And the court had to direct him to the relevant provisions of the rules, including the definition of the “date of filing”. He then turned and belatedly sought condonation. But he sought that I condone something that is a nullity. It was not that he intended to file valid pleadings. As alluded to above, the court cannot be asked to condone a nullity. The court is not enjoined to inquire into other issues, including the requirements for granting condonation, where the condonation was sought to have a nullity accepted as properly filed. It must be emphasised that the rules of court are very important in the administration of justice. Any litigant who disregards the rules of court may find him/herself non-suited. Thus, in Museredza & Ors v Minister of Agriculture, Lands, Water and Rural Resettlement & Ors CCZ 01/22 at p 14 para 27 GOWORA JCC said: 
“It goes without saying that rules of court serve an important purpose. They must not, and, cannot be disregarded unless the court has been persuaded, on very strong grounds, to do so. This is why a litigant who wishes to exercise the right to access the court is required to follow certain and specified procedures. In this context, rules of court not only ensure certainty of the processes of court proceedings, the litigants are also provided with a fair playing ground for the adjudication of disputes. It is fair to state that the rules ensure that the interests of justice are served.” (my emphasis)    
	Consequently, in light of the principles set out in our law, there is nothing before me from the respondents. There is also nothing to condone, as the court cannot condone a nullity. Their purported notice of opposition and opposing affidavit being a nullity are accordingly struck out or expunged from the record, and the matter shall proceed as unopposed.
THE UNOPPOSED APPLICATION
The application was now unopposed. After hearing Mr Mucheche, I saw no reason not to grant the order sought in the draft order attached to the application. The court accordingly entered judgment in favour of the applicant, with no order as to costs, as fully stated above. 
DEMBURE J: ……………………………………………
Caleb Mucheche & Partners Law Chambers, applicant’s legal practitioners
Civil Division of the Attorney-General’s Office, respondents’ legal practitioners
